## Definition

A polar covalent bond is a covalent bond in which the sharing of electrons between atoms is unequal, so that one atom attracts the shared electrons more than the other.

## Core Principles

- The unequal sharing of electrons in a polar covalent bond arises from differences in *electronegativity* between the bonded atoms; the atom with greater electronegativity attracts the shared electrons more strongly.

## Sub-concepts

- **Electronegativity**: the power of an atom to attract shared electrons toward itself within a covalent bond.
- **Molecular Formulae and Structures** (section 3.2.3): representations of how atoms combine and are arranged within molecules.

## Field-level Controversies

_(尚無內容)_

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

_(尚無內容)_

## Practical Applications

- Water (H₂O) is an important example of a molecule containing polar covalent bonds: the oxygen atom has greater electronegativity than the two hydrogen atoms and therefore attracts the shared electrons more strongly.
